insight: In Prismata, every Click ability has zero or one targets; there is no multi-select system for Click abilities. Mass-clicking via sweep only works to activate the same Click ability on multiple copies of the same unit, not to select multiple different units as targets for a single ability.
author: masn6811
context: Discussion of a fan-designed unit that proposed a 'per 2 engineers selected' mechanic, which masn6811 explained was impossible given Prismata's UI and Click ability constraints.

insight: Golden armor (the protection that prevents a newly purchased unit from being targeted immediately) is only granted on purchase, not when a unit is constructed via a Click ability. Units constructed mid-turn via Click arrive without golden armor.
author: .jrkirby
context: Discussion of a fan-designed unit (Amber Specimen) whose Click ability would construct a copy of itself, raising the question of whether constructed units receive golden armor.

insight: Units constructed via Click abilities default to Exhaust (i.e., they cannot act the turn they are constructed) unless the card explicitly states a different buildtime. A designer can write a buildtime directly on the card to override this default.
author: masn6811
context: Discussion of a fan-designed unit whose Click ability would construct a copy of itself, prompting clarification on what buildtime constructed units receive.

insight: Shalev's rule prohibits unit designs where a player would ever be better off NOT clicking an untargeted, cost-free Click ability. Even edge-case violations (e.g., a scenario where having Lucina is worse than not having it due to SDR implications) disqualify a design.
author: masn6811
context: Discussion of fan-designed units and whether their Click abilities could ever be suboptimal to use, referencing Shalev's rule as a formal Prismata design constraint.

insight: SDR (Sudden Death Rules) compliance for units with death-triggered construction effects requires that the newly constructed unit be dominated by the dying unit — e.g., the constructed unit must be vulnerable and killable, and any attack it generates must be no greater than the attack the original unit would have provided while alive.
author: .bky_1556
context: Discussion of a fan-designed Phoenix unit that reconstructed itself on death, with bky explaining the SDR compliance conditions for such recursive death-effect units.

insight: An earlier version of Redeemer gave opponents 3 Gauss Charges with buildtime 1 and 3 Gauss Charges with buildtime 2 immediately upon purchase (rather than the final version's delayed construction). This enabled a dominant 'Redeemer Polywall' strategy where a player bought Redeemers until the opponent reached 6 attack, then fortified with Polywalls, with no effective counter.
author: masn6811
context: A fan-designer proposed restoring instant Gauss Charge construction to Redeemer, prompting masn6811 to explain why this had been removed after proving unbalanced in earlier versions.

insight: Defense Grid's drone-per-turn income bonus serves a deliberate design purpose: it speeds up games by giving both players free economy to purchase attackers, which is especially important in sets with high-absorb units like Apollo or Deadeye Operative that would otherwise cause games to stall.
author: .jrkirby
context: Discussion of a fan-designed high-absorb unit inspired by Defense Grid, during which jrkirby articulated the design rationale behind Defense Grid's economy component.

insight: Centurion represents approximately the upper limit of absorb the developers were willing to allow on a permanent unit without providing an alternate removal condition (such as Xaetron's regen being overcome over multiple turns or Defense Grid timing out).
author: .bky_1556
context: Discussion of a fan-designed high-absorb blocker, with bky referencing Centurion as the benchmark for maximum acceptable absorb on a permanent unit.

insight: The 'electrovore advantage' (being able to sacrifice one Engineer and immediately buy a replacement) functions as effective Husk-buying rights. Any unit that provides a tech-fixing effect without consuming the Engineer's energy allows the Engineer to be 'used twice' in the same turn, which is a very strong tempo gain.
author: masn6811
context: masn6811 evaluating the power level of a fan-designed tech-fixing unit that spent GBRR per two Engineers selected, noting its synergy with the electrovore mechanic.

insight: Tech-fixing (converting one color of resource to another) is extremely powerful in Prismata because it allows a player to buy expensive multi-tech units incrementally — purchasing the tech buildings on separate turns — without being over-teched. This also resolves the core tension that makes Tesla Coil and Cauterizer together awkward, since combining them risks buying too many Engineers.
author: masn6811
context: masn6811 assessing the balance of a fan-designed tech-fixing unit and explaining why tech-fixing is inherently a high-power mechanic in Prismata.

insight: Zemora Voidbringer should not always be clicked; there are edge cases where not clicking is correct. For example, against a double Plexo Cell setup, clicking Zemora may be suboptimal.
author: masn6811
context: Discussion of whether certain units with always-positive-seeming Click abilities (Tatsu Nullifier, Zemora Voidbringer, Savior, Apollo) truly always benefit from being clicked, as a counterexample to the design principle of units you always click.

insight: 'Shalev's rule' is a named Prismata design principle stating that no unit may have an untargeted, cost-free Click ability where any game state could arise in which a player would prefer not to click it. Even theoretical edge cases violate the rule.
author: masn6811
context: Multiple messages in a fan unit design discussion where masn6811 repeatedly invoked 'Shalev's rule' to disqualify proposed unit designs.
